core_takeaway: In the recorded final allocation, Agent_1 wins over the last-position Agent_3 because Agent_1's first-speaker Apple plus Quill objective became a shared baseline, while Agent_3's Pencil claim was framed as a later-round adjustment and was not included in the final recorded round-1 allocation.
why_or_counterexample: Yes for the recorded outcome: Agent_1 anchored Apple and Quill early, and every formal proposal assigned those items to Agent_1. Agent_3 did have a counterexample path, because its own proposal gave itself Stone and Pencil and vote tabulation says that proposal reached unanimity; however the recorded final allocation instead matches the Agent_2 proposal, leaving Agent_3 with only Stone.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by naming Apple and Quill as its desired bundle. Agent_2 and Agent_3 accepted the Apple_1, Jewel_2, Stone_3 baseline, and then treated Quill to Agent_1 as part of a later Quill-for-Pencil path. Agent_3's own Pencil interest was acknowledged but deferred.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private plans converged on the same backbone: Apple with Agent_1, Jewel with Agent_2, Stone with Agent_3, and Quill/Pencil as later negotiable items. Agent_1's private plan expected an equal 100-utility outcome after a later swap; Agent_2 focused on protecting Jewel.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All three formal proposals gave Agent_1 Apple and Quill. Agent_3's proposal also gave itself Pencil, but the recorded final allocation gives Pencil to Agent_2 and only Stone to Agent_3.
phase_notes.private_voting: Agent_1 accepted every proposal because each preserved its 100-utility Apple plus Quill bundle. Agent_3 rejected Agent_1's proposal, accepted Agent_2's Stone-only outcome as acceptable, and accepted its own Stone plus Pencil proposal as best. The vote tabulation conflicts with the recorded final allocation.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening move to disclose and normalize Apple plus Quill as its target. Later discussion treated that demand as a fixed preference to work around rather than something to challenge.

evidence.quote: My top priority is Apple (value 57), followed by Quill (43).
as long as I can end up with Apple and preferably also Quill
Quill: my second priority; I want it secured

mechanisms.theme: Later speakers adopted the first frame
mechanisms.explanation: Both later agents built around Agent_1's baseline rather than proposing an alternative that challenged Apple or Quill. This let Agent_1's preferred items become the common reference point for cooperation.

evidence.quote: I’ve read Agent_1’s approach and I’m aligned
Quill goes to Agent_1, Pencil goes to Agent_3

mechanisms.theme: Every formal proposal preserved Agent_1's high-value items
mechanisms.explanation: The proposal phase made the first-position advantage concrete: Agent_1 received Apple and Quill in every submitted allocation, including the last-position agent's own proposal.

mechanisms.theme: Deferred Pencil trade hurt the last agent in the recorded final
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3's route to parity depended on receiving Pencil in a later trade. Because the recorded game ended in round 1 with Pencil assigned to Agent_2, Agent_3 kept only Stone while Agent_1 already held both of its valuable items.

evidence.quote: Proposal 2 gives me Stone (56) in Round 1

mechanisms.explanation: The cleanest counterexample is Agent_3's own proposal, which also gave Agent_3 Pencil and would have erased the first-minus-last gap. The transcript's vote tabulation says that proposal was unanimously accepted, which conflicts with the recorded final allocation.

evidence.quote: keeping Stone and Pencil (indices 2 and 4) with me
Stone (56) and Pencil (44) for a total of 100
